{"type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"toolu_011mnTovmkURbXcCvsaXxTZ2","type":"tool_result","content":"   900→\t\t\t   \"polling the codec once: last cmd=0x%08x\\n\",\n   901→\t\t\t   chip->last_cmd[addr]);\n   902→\t\tdo_poll = 1;\n   903→\t\tchip->poll_count++;\n   904→\t\tgoto again;\n   905→\t}\n   906→\n   907→\n   908→\tif (!chip->polling_mode) {\n   909→\t\tsnd_printk(KERN_WARNING SFX \"azx_get_response timeout, \"\n   910→\t\t\t   \"switching to polling mode: last cmd=0x%08x\\n\",\n   911→\t\t\t   chip->last_cmd[addr]);\n   912→\t\tchip->polling_mode = 1;\n   913→\t\tgoto again;\n   914→\t}\n   915→\n   916→\tif (chip->msi) {\n   917→\t\tsnd_printk(KERN_WARNING SFX \"No response from codec, \"\n   918→\t\t\t   \"disabling MSI: last cmd=0x%08x\\n\",\n   919→\t\t\t   chip->last_cmd[addr]);\n   920→\t\tfree_irq(chip->irq, chip);\n   921→\t\tchip->irq = -1;\n   922→\t\tpci_disable_msi(chip->pci);\n   923→\t\tchip->msi = 0;\n   924→\t\tif (azx_acquire_irq(chip, 1) < 0) {\n   925→\t\t\tbus->rirb_error = 1;\n   926→\t\t\treturn -1;\n   927→\t\t}\n   928→\t\tgoto again;\n   929→\t}\n   930→\n   931→\tif (chip->probing) {\n   932→\t\t/* If this critical timeout happens during the codec probing\n   933→\t\t * phase, this is likely an access to a non-existing codec\n   934→\t\t * slot. bits 0-2 of PCI register TCSEL (at offset 0x44)\n\t * TCSEL == Traffic Class Select Register, which sets PCI express QOS\n\t * Ensuring these bits are 0 clears playback static on some HD Audio\n\t * Let me trace through what happens:\n\n1. Initial state: single_cmd = 0, polling_mode = 0\n2. Device enters suspend, azx_suspend calls azx_stop_chip which sets initialized = 0\n3. Device resumes, azx_resume calls azx_init_chip which should re-initialize everything\n4. But azx_init_chip doesn't reset single_cmd and polling_mode!\n\nSo if the device got into an error state where single_cmd and/or polling_mode were set to 1 before suspend, they would still be 1 after resume.\n\nBut wait, looking at azx_suspend more carefully:\n```c\nstatic int azx_suspend(struct device *dev)\n{\n    ...\n    azx_stop_chip(chip);  // This sets initialized = 0\n    ...\n}\n```\n\nAnd azx_resume:\n```c\nstatic int azx_resume(struct device *dev)\n{\n    ...\n    azx_init_pci(chip);\n    azx_init_chip(chip, 1);  // This checks if initialized and returns early if true\n    snd_hda_resume(chip->bus);\n    ...\n}\n```\n\nSo azx_init_chip is called with chip->initialized = 0, and it should initialize. But the issue is that azx_init_chip doesn't reset the polling_mode and single_cmd flags!\n\nWait, let me re-check the azx_resume code. Looking at line 2554-2556:\n```c\nif (chip->msi)\n    if (pci_enable_msi(pci) < 0)\n        chip->msi = 0;\n```\n\nThis tries to re-enable MSI if it was previously enabled. But the polling_mode and single_cmd flags aren't being reset during resume. The problem is that if these flags were set during error handling before suspend, they persist after resume. The fix should reset these flags in azx_resume before calling azx_init_chip, or azx_init_chip itself should clear them when reinitializing.\n\nLooking at the code flow: if single_cmd mode or polling_mode were activated during error recovery before suspend, they need to be cleared on resume. The poll_count variable should also be reset. {"type":"assistant","message":{"model":"claude-haiku-4-5-20251001","id":"msg_013MHX3Wny7NSb1CMWu86Cve","type":"message","role":"assistant","content":[{"type":"thinking","thinking":"Perfect! Now I fully understand the bug:\n\nLooking at azx_rirb_get_response (line 863):\n- Line 898-905: If polling_mode is not set and poll_count < 2, it tries polling once\n- Line 908-913: If polling_mode is still not set (after first poll), it sets polling_mode = 1 and tries again\n- Line 916-928: If still no response and MSI is enabled, it disables MSI and tries again- Line 948-957: If still no response, it sets single_cmd = 1 and disables CORB/RIRB\n\nSo the progression is:\n1. polling_mode = 0, poll_count = 0 → try polling once (poll_count becomes 1)\n2. polling_mode = 0, poll_count = 1 → try polling second time (poll_count becomes 2)\n3. polling_mode = 0, poll_count = 2+ → set polling_mode = 1 (enable always-on polling)\n4. polling_mode = 1 but still no response and MSI enabled → disable MSI\n5. polling_mode = 1 and MSI disabled but still no response → set single_cmd = 1\n\nNow, what happens after suspend/resume?\n\nDuring suspend:\n- azx_suspend calls azx_stop_chip which sets initialized = 0\n\nDuring resume:\n- azx_init_chip is called with chip->initialized = 0\n- azx_init_chip does:\n  - azx_reset\n  - azx_int_clear, azx_int_enable\n  - azx_init_cmd_io (if single_cmd is 0)\n  - Sets initialized = 1\n\nTHE BUG: If single_cmd was set to 1 before suspend due to error handling, then:\n- On resume, at line 1222: `if (!chip->single_cmd)` means azx_init_cmd_io is NOT called\n\n\n- CORB/RIRB stays disabled since it was turned off during error recovery, leaving the device unable to communicate properly. The same issue applies to polling_mode—if it was set to 1 before suspend, it persists through resume and can cause ongoing problems. The solution would be to clear these error-recovery flags either in azx_resume before reinitializing the chip, or at the start of azx_init_chip itself.\n\nLooking at line 1222, the condition `if (!chip->single_cmd)` determines whether CORB/RIRB gets reinitialized—setting single_cmd to 1 skips this initialization entirely. On resume, we need CORB/RIRB to reinitialize regardless of whether single_cmd was set during error handling, so resetting it beforehand is necessary. The polling_mode flag follows a similar pattern where poll_count gets reset to 0 on successful responses when do_poll isn't active, but the core issue remains that these flags need to be cleared on resume to restore normal operation. from the code structure. When `single_cmd` gets set to 1 during error handling before suspend, it persists through the resume cycle since `azx_init_chip` checks this flag at line 1222 to decide whether to reinitialize CORB/RIRB. If the flag isn't cleared, the command ring and response ring won't be reinitialized after resume, breaking communication with the device. The fix would need to reset these state flags in the resume path to ensure proper reinitialization.","signature":""}],"stop_reason":null,"stop_sequence":null,"usage":{"input_tokens":8,"cache_creation_input_tokens":2855,"cache_read_input_tokens":36213,"cache_creation":{"ephemeral_5m_input_tokens":0,"ephemeral_1h_input_tokens":2855},"output_tokens":1,"service_tier":"standard","inference_geo":"not_available"},"context_management":null},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"3626ef2b-4a35-4abf-bd29-c343bb397ce6","uuid":"4ea31120-06c4-4d53-ab27-17df53b6d649"}

{"type":"assistant","message":{"model":"claude-haiku-4-5-20251001","id":"msg_018NWMMdGMLdKG89pELqb8PP","type":"message","role":"assistant","content":[{"type":"thinking","thinking":"Git history didn't show anything useful. Let me analyze the code myself.The key issue is in azx_resume. When the chip resumes after suspend, it should reset the error-handling flags (single_cmd, polling_mode) to their initial state before reinitializing the chip.\n\nLet me check what happens in azx_resume. Looking at the code:\n\nazx_resume (line 2539):\n```c\nstatic int azx_resume(struct device *dev)\n{\n    ...\n    pci_set_master(pci);\n    if (chip->msi)\n        if (pci_enable_msi(pci) < 0)\n            chip->msi = 0;\n    if (azx_acquire_irq(chip, 1) < 0)\n        return -EIO;\n    azx_init_pci(chip);\n\n    azx_init_chip(chip, 1);  // <-- This is where the chip is re-initialized\n\n    snd_hda_resume(chip->bus);\n    ...\n}\n```\n\nAnd azx_init_chip:\n```c\nstatic void azx_init_chip(struct azx *chip, int full_reset)\n{\n    if (chip->initialized)\n        return;\n\n    /* reset controller */\n    azx_reset(chip, full_reset);\n\n    /* initialize interrupts */\n    azx_int_clear(chip);\n    azx_int_enable(chip);\n\n    /* initialize the codec command I/O */\n    if (!chip->single_cmd)\n        azx_init_cmd_io(chip);  // <-- CORB/RIRB initialization is skipped if single_cmd = 1\n\n    ...\n    chip->initialized = 1;\n}\n```\n\nSo the problem is:\n1. If single_cmd was set to 1 during error handling before suspend\n2. azx_init_chip won't call azx_init_cmd_io to re-initialize CORB/RIRB3. So the device is stuck in single_cmd mode after resumeThe fix could be:Option A: Reset single_cmd in azx_resume before calling azx_init_chip\n\n\nOption B: Reset single_cmd at the beginning of azx_init_chip\nOption C: Force azx_init_chip to reinitialize CORB/RIRB even if single_cmd = 1\n\nOption A seems cleanest—clearing error-handling flags during resume before reinitializing the chip. Since azx_init_chip is called from multiple places, modifying its behavior globally could have unintended side effects. I should also check polling_mode to see if it needs resetting as well, since it's another fallback mode that might be set during error handling.\n\nLooking at the code, polling_mode affects how azx_rirb_get_response behaves, and leaving it enabled could cause unnecessary active polling. The poll_count variable also needs attention—it's only reset when responses succeed, so if the device suspends during an error state, poll_count might remain non-zero. The fix should reset both single_cmd and polling_mode in azx_resume before reinitializing, and possibly poll_count too. I'm considering whether azx_init_chip itself should handle this after the initialized check, or if the real issue is that these flags aren't being cleared properly during the suspend-resume cycle.\n\nThe core problem seems to be that when error handling puts the device into single_cmd mode, it needs to transition back to normal CORB/RIRB operation on resume. This transition requires clearing the single_cmd flag before re-initialization happens. So the fix belongs in azx_resume—resetting single_cmd, polling_mode, and poll_count to their default states before calling azx_init_chip will ensure the device doesn't stay stuck in error-handling mode after waking up. chip (line 1209) - the check at line 1222 should maybe be modified, or the single_cmd flag should always be reset on resume. azx_stop_chip (line 2432) might also need to reset single_cmd and polling_mode, though that seems less critical.","signature":""}],"stop_reason":null,"stop_sequence":null,"usage":{"input_tokens":9,"cache_creation_input_tokens":1326,"cache_read_input_tokens":39068,"cache_creation":{"ephemeral_5m_input_tokens":0,"ephemeral_1h_input_tokens":1326},"output_tokens":1,"service_tier":"standard","inference_geo":"not_available"},"context_management":null},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"3626ef2b-4a35-4abf-bd29-c343bb397ce6","uuid":"b475278c-4514-4d31-adc4-3baa62f9b58b"}

Tries to re-enable MSI (and sets chip->msi = 0 if it fails)\n3. Acquires IRQ\n4. Calls azx_init_pci\n5. Calls azx_init_chip\n\nBut it DOES NOT reset the single_cmd and polling_mode flags!\n\nIf these flags were set during error handling before suspend, they remain set after resume. Then when azx_init_chip is called:- Line 1222: `if (!chip->single_cmd)` will be false (because single_cmd = 1)\n- azx_init_cmd_io will NOT be called\n- CORB/RIRB will not be re-initialized\n- The device remains in single_cmd mode\n\nThis causes the codec communication issues described in the bug report.\n\nThe fix is to reset these flags in azx_resume before calling azx_init_chip:\n```c\nchip->single_cmd = 0;\nchip->polling_mode = 0;\nchip->poll_count = 0;\n```\n\nOr alternatively, reset them in azx_init_chip at the beginning (after the if (chip->initialized) check).\n\nLet me also check the runtime suspend/resume functions to see if they have the same issue.","signature":""}],"stop_reason":null,"stop_sequence":null,"usage":{"input_tokens":7,"cache_creation_input_tokens":870,"cache_read_input_tokens":41970,"cache_creation":{"ephemeral_5m_input_tokens":0,"ephemeral_1h_input_tokens":870},"output_tokens":3,"service_tier":"standard","inference_geo":"not_available"},"context_management":null},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"3626ef2b-4a35-4abf-bd29-c343bb397ce6","uuid":"85513d5b-68e8-422b-9f38-3bd1dd40722c"}

{"type":"assistant","message":{"model":"claude-haiku-4-5-20251001","id":"msg_01CteNbGiv8agfGZYKTQZWnk","type":"message","role":"assistant","content":[{"type":"thinking","thinking":"So line 1670 is also in a reset path during codec probe. This is another place where single_cmd and polling_mode should be reset.\n\nLet me now think about the best place to put the fix. All these callers of azx_init_chip are doing a reset/re-initialization scenario where we want to start fresh. So it makes sense to reset the error-handling flags in azx_init_chip itself, right after the check for chip->initialized.\n\nActually, let me reconsider. The check `if (chip->initialized) return;` at line 1211-1212 means that azx_init_chip only does something if chip->initialized is 0. And chip->initialized is set to 0 by azx_stop_chip (line 2448). So whenever azx_init_chip is called after azx_stop_chip, it will actually do the initialization.\n\nSo the fix could be:Option A: Reset flags in azx_init_chip after the if (chip->initialized) check (before the actual initialization)\nOption B: Reset flags in azx_resume and azx_runtime_resume before calling azx_init_chipOption C: Reset flags in azx_resume and azx_runtime_resume and also in azx_bus_reset and the probe error path\n\nI think Option A is cleanest - reset the flags in azx_init_chip right after the if (chip->initialized) check, because:1. All error recovery paths go through azx_init_chip2. It's the logical place to reset error-handling flags before reinitializing3. It ensures consistency across all callers\n\nBut wait, I need to be careful. Let me check again if the single_cmd module parameter should be preserved.\n\nLooking at line 3074:\n```cchip->single_cmd = single_cmd;\n```\nThis is in some initialization function (probably probe), setting single_cmd from the module parameter. So the module parameter is preserved.\n\nBut in azx_init_chip, we're doing a re-initialization where we want to reset the error-handling state. \n\nThe key issue is that single_cmd gets modified during error handling (line 951), but we need to restore it to the original module parameter value on re-initialization, not just reset it to 0. The module parameter value is already stored in chip->single_cmd at probe time, so on resume we should restore it to that original value rather than leaving it in whatever error state it ended up in. The error handling path at line 951 can overwrite the initial module parameter value. The real question is whether to restore single_cmd to its original module parameter setting on resume, or attempt CORB/RIRB communication again regardless of how it was originally set.\n\nGiven the bug—where the device loses codec communication after suspend/resume—the fix should reset the error state and give the hardware another chance to communicate properly. So polling_mode and poll_count should definitely be cleared. For single_cmd, since there's no way to track whether it came from the module parameter or from error recovery, resetting it to 0 on resume makes sense to let the device attempt normal operation again.\n\nBut there's a complication: if single_cmd was set to 1 via module parameter during probe, we'd be overriding the user's explicit configuration by resetting it. The probe function sets chip->single_cmd from the module parameter initially, so by the time azx_init_chip runs, it has the correct value. If error handling later changes it to 1, we need to distinguish that from the original parameter value on resume. Without tracking the source of the setting, we can't know whether single_cmd = 1 came from the user's module parameter or from error recovery.\n\nThe solution is to reference the global module parameter variable directly—it's available as `single_cmd` and gets copied to chip->single_cmd at probe time, so we could restore from that source during resume.\n\nHowever, there's a complication: if a user explicitly loaded the module with single_cmd=1, we shouldn't override that setting. The fix should reset single_cmd back to 0 on resume and let the device attempt CORB/RIRB mode again, falling back to single_cmd mode only if needed.\n\nThe simplest approach is resetting single_cmd to the module parameter value on resume, along with clearing the error-handling flags like polling_mode and poll_count. This way the device gets a fresh attempt at normal operation rather than staying stuck in the fallback mode.\n\nThe core issue is that once these flags are set during error recovery, they persist across suspend/resume cycles. Resetting them ensures the device can attempt normal operation again instead of remaining in the degraded state. The fix should happen early in the initialization sequence, before the actual chip setup begins. calls azx_stop_chip (sets initialized = 0). Then azx_resume calls azx_init_chip, which checks if chip->initialized is already set and returns early if so. That's the actual bug. This leaves the device stuck in single_cmd mode, causing codec communication failures that manifest as choppy sound with 2-second silences. The device only recovers after a full reboot (which clears all state). {"type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"toolu_01EcXc49bQsMxVUz9fRPagMD","type":"tool_result","content":"     1→{\n     2→  \"buggy_files\": [\n     3→    \"sound/pci/hda/hda_intel.c\"\n     4→  ],\n     5→  \"buggy_functions\": [\n     6→    \"azx_resume\",\n     7→    \"azx_runtime_resume\"\n     8→  ],\n     9→  \"confidence\": 0.95,\n    10→  \"reasoning\": \"The HDA Intel driver encounters communication errors during codec probing or operation, which trigger error-handling code that sets chip->single_cmd=1 and/or chip->polling_mode=1. When the device suspends and resumes, azx_resume() and azx_runtime_resume() call azx_init_chip() to re-initialize the hardware. However, they do not reset the error-handling flags (single_cmd, polling_mode, poll_count) before re-initialization. In azx_init_chip(), there is a critical check at line 1222: 'if (!chip->single_cmd)' which only initializes CORB/RIRB if single_cmd=0. Since the error-handling code set single_cmd=1 and this flag is never reset on resume, the CORB/RIRB command interface is not re-initialized after resume. This leaves the device stuck in single_cmd mode, causing codec communication failures that manifest as choppy sound with 2-second silences. The device only recovers after a full reboot (which clears all state). The fix is to reset these flags in azx_resume() and azx_runtime_resume() before calling azx_init_chip().\"\n    11→}\n    12→\n\n<system-reminder>\nWhenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. You CAN and SHOULD provide analysis of malware, what it is doing. But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior.\n</system-reminder>\n"}]},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"3626ef2b-4a35-4abf-bd29-c343bb397ce6","uuid":"001c14fb-ef34-4b85-9e22-92ed7072b3c6","tool_use_result":{"type":"text","file":{"filePath":"/workspace/fault_localization_result.json","content":"{\n  \"buggy_files\": [\n    \"sound/pci/hda/hda_intel.c\"\n  ],\n  \"buggy_functions\": [\n    \"azx_resume\",\n    \"azx_runtime_resume\"\n  ],\n  \"confidence\": 0.95,\n  \"reasoning\": \"The HDA Intel driver encounters communication errors during codec probing or operation, which trigger error-handling code that sets chip->single_cmd=1 and/or chip->polling_mode=1. When the device suspends and resumes, azx_resume() and azx_runtime_resume() call azx_init_chip() to re-initialize the hardware. However, they do not reset the error-handling flags (single_cmd, polling_mode, poll_count) before re-initialization. In azx_init_chip(), there is a critical check at line 1222: 'if (!chip->single_cmd)' which only initializes CORB/RIRB if single_cmd=0. Since the error-handling code set single_cmd=1 and this flag is never reset on resume, the CORB/RIRB command interface is not re-initialized after resume. This leaves the device stuck in single_cmd mode, causing codec communication failures that manifest as choppy sound with 2-second silences. The device only recovers after a full reboot (which clears all state). The fix is to reset these flags in azx_resume() and azx_runtime_resume() before calling azx_init_chip().\"\n}\n","numLines":12,"startLine":1,"totalLines":12}}}

{"type":"assistant","message":{"model":"claude-haiku-4-5-20251001","id":"msg_011UgNgBhLxU95MAavVTd58u","type":"message","role":"assistant","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Perfect! Let me create a summary of my findings:\n\n## Fault Localization Summary\n\nI've identified the root cause of the HDA Intel sound driver failure after suspend to RAM:\n\n### **Buggy File**\n- `sound/pci/hda/hda_intel.c`\n\n### **Buggy Functions**\n1. `azx_resume()` (line 2539) - System suspend/resume handler\n2. `azx_runtime_resume()` (line 2580) - Runtime PM resume handler\n\n### **Root Cause**\n\nThe bug occurs due to a missing reset of error-handling flags during device resume:\n\n1. **Error Handling Phase**: When codec communication timeouts occur, `azx_rirb_get_response()` progressively enables error-handling modes:\n   - Sets `polling_mode = 1` (line 912)\n   - Disables MSI (line 923)\n   - Sets `single_cmd = 1` (line 951) and calls `azx_free_cmd_io()` to disable CORB/RIRB\n\n2. **Suspend Phase**: `azx_suspend()` calls `azx_stop_chip()` which sets `initialized = 0`\n\n3. **Resume Phase** (THE BUG): `azx_resume()` and `azx_runtime_resume()` call `azx_init_chip()` to re-initialize, BUT:\n   - They do NOT reset `single_cmd`, `polling_mode`, or `poll_count` flags\n   - In `azx_init_chip()` at line 1222, there's a critical check: `if (!chip->single_cmd)`\n   - Since `single_cmd = 1` from error handling, CORB/RIRB is NOT re-initialized\n   - Device remains stuck in single_cmd mode, causing codec communication failures\n\n4. **Result**: Choppy audio with 2-second silences after resume. Only solution is reboot (which clears all state).\n\n### **Confidence: 0.95**\n\nThe result has been written to `/workspace/fault_localization_result.json`."}],"stop_reason":null,"stop_sequence":null,"usage":{"input_tokens":5,"cache_creation_input_tokens":857,"cache_read_input_tokens":52649,"cache_creation":{"ephemeral_5m_input_tokens":0,"ephemeral_1h_input_tokens":857},"output_tokens":3,"service_tier":"standard","inference_geo":"not_available"},"context_management":null},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"3626ef2b-4a35-4abf-bd29-c343bb397ce6","uuid":"b3fcb452-b5b9-4ea3-b2de-2dd8e5834fa0"}
